title: Conducting polymer-based electrochemical redox supercapacitors using proton and lithium ion conducting polymer electrolytes
abstract:
Polymer International 47 (1998) 28È33
Conducting Polymer-based
Electrochemical Redox Supercapacitors
Using Proton and Lithium Ion Conducting
Polymer Electrolytes
S. A. Hashmi,¤ R. J. Latham, R. G. Linford & W. S. Schlindwein*
Solid State Research Centre, School of Applied Sciences, De Montfort University, The Gateway, Leicester, LE1 9BH, UK
(Received 5 September 1997; accepted 21 January 1998)
Abstract: Polypyrrole-based solid-state redox supercapacitors have been con-
structed
using
proton
and
lithium
ion
conducting
polymer
electrolytes:
poly(vinyl alcohol)
and poly(ethylene oxide)
(PVA)ÈH3PO4 ,
(PEO)ÈLiCF3SO3
plasticized with poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG). The capacitors have been character-
ized using a.c. impedance, cyclic linear sweep voltammetry and galvanostatic
chargeÈdischarge methods. Redox capacitors based on polypyrrole show large
values of capacitance (about 1É5È5É0 mF cm~2) (equivalent to a single electrode
capacitance of 40È84 F g~1 of polypyrrole) for both the electrolytes. The values
of capacitance have been found to be stable up to 1000 chargeÈdischarge cycles
between 0 and 1É0 V.
1998 Society of Chemical Industry
(
Polym. Int. 47, 28È33 (1998)
Key words: conducting polymers; supercapacitors; polymer electrolytes
INTRODUCTION
Over the past few years, a growing interest in the devel-
opment of electrochemical supercapacitors has arisen
because of their possible potential applications in elec-
tronic equipment, medical devices and electrical vehi-
cles.1h4 One of the most promising applications of
supercapacitors is in hybrid power design for electrical
vehicles where they may be used as a complementary
power system to the main batteries. The main battery
* To whom all correspondence should be addressed.
¤ On sabbatical leave from the Department of Physics, North
Eastern Regional Institute of Science and Technology, Itana-
gar (Nirjuli), Arunachal Pradesh-791 109, India.
Contract/grant sponsor: Government of India.
Contract/grant number: BOYSCAST Fellowship.
system provides power for continuous motion, whereas
supercapacitors (connected in parallel to the batteries)
ensure additional high-power delivery for acceleration.
The conÐguration of supercapacitors is almost similar
to that of batteries, the di†erence being that the former
consist of electrolytes sandwiched between two blocking
or active electrodes with translation of charge only,
whereas a battery employs asymmetrical di†erent elec-
trode materials involved in interfacial reactions leading
to an overall electrochemical process for the cell.
On the basis of electrode materials used an the charge
storage mechanisms, electrochemical supercapacitors
are classiÐed as: (a) electrical double-layer capacitors
(EDLCs)
which
employ
carbon
or
other
similar
materials as blocking electrodes,5h8 and (b) redox
supercapacitors in which electroactive materials such as
28

 Electrochemical redox supercapacitors
29
insertion type compounds (e.g.
NiO, etc.) or con-
RuO2 ,
ducting polymers are employed as electrodes.9h18 For
EDLCs particularly, activated carbons in the form of
Ðbres or phenolic resin-based fabrics are most attractive
because of their large surface area, typically 1000È
2500 m2 g~1, which correspond to very high speciÐc
capacitance
values
of
200È500 F g~1.
The
charge
storage mechanism in EDLCs is electrostatic in origin,
which involves the formation of an electrical double
layer
due
to
charge
separation
at
the
electrodeÈ
electrolyte interfaces. In redox supercapacitors based on
either
electrode materials9 or conducting
RuO2-type
polymers,10h18 a faradic charge transfer process takes
place which leads to the pseudocapacitance at the inter-
faces.
In
particular,
conducting
polymers
represent
an
attractive class of materials for use as electrodes in elec-
trochemical capacitors because of advantageous proper-
ties
which
include
fast
dopingÈdedoping
during
chargeÈdischarge, high charge density, easy chemical/
electrochemical synthesis and low cost as compared to
noble metal oxides.11,12,14 Rudge et al.14,15 have pro-
posed three possible schemes by which conducting poly-
mers can be used as electrodes in redox supercapacitors.
In type I capacitors, two identical electrodes comprising
symmetrical
and
identical
p-dopable
conducting
polymer Ðlms are used, whereas two di†erent p-dopable
conducting polymers are used in type II capacitors.
Type II capacitors can be charged to a comparatively
higher voltage by virtue of the di†erence in the potential
range over which conducting polymers can be doped
and dedoped. A mix of both n- and p-doped conducting
polymers can be used in type III capacitors which are
able to achieve the highest possible voltage (typically
about 3 V) in the charged state.
Many redox supercapacitors based on liquid electro-
lytes have been reported,11,12,14h16 but these are associ-
ated with the well-known disadvantage of corrosion, self
discharge, low energy density and bulky design. Cur-
rently, a great deal of attention has been focused on the
design of new all solid-state redox supercapacitors
based on conducting polymer electrodes and solid
polymer electrolytes. All solid-state redox capacitors
have not been widely reported except for a few recent
studies which include di†erent polymeric/gel electro-
lytes, e.g. poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)/ethylene
carbonate
(EC)/propylene
carbonate
(PC)/LiClO4 ,
10,13,17
poly(ethylene
glycol)
(PEG)/PC/tetraethyl-
ammonium tetraÑuoroborate
etc.
(TEABF4),18
This paper presents the results from studies on all
solid-state redox capacitors fabricated from polypyrrole
electrodes (electrodeposited on indiumÈtin oxide (ITO)
conducting glass and carbon sheet) with a polymer elec-
trolyte. The polymer electrolytes were based on proton
and
lithium
ion
conducting
polymeric
systems
poly(vinyl alcohol)
and poly(ethylene
(PVA)ÈH3PO4
oxide) [PEO]Èpoly(ethylene glycol) (PEG)ÈLiCF3SO3 ,
respectively. The performance characteristics of the
capacitors have been evaluated by a.c. impedance, linear
sweep
voltammetry
and
cyclic
chargeÈdischarge
methods.

Details of the preparation method of polymer electro-
lytes have been published by us elsewhere.8
The polypyrrole was electrochemically deposited on
two substrates: (a) indiumÈtin oxide coated conducting
glass (ITO, sheet resistance about 80 ) cm2, Balzers)
and (b) high density graphite sheets (C, 250 lm thick,
approximately 0É027 g cm~2, FMI Composite Ltd.). The
monomer pyrrole (Aldrich) and the acetonitrile solvent
(Aldrich) were distilled before use. Electrosyntheses were
carried out in a one-compartment, three-electrode cell
containing 0É05 M pyrrole and
solutions
0É1 M LiClO4
in acetonitrile. The solutions were degassed by purging
with dry nitrogen before electropolymerization. The
electrodepositions were made on ITO with a constant
current of 1 mA for 10 min and on carbon sheets with a
current of 6 mA for 4 min. The deposition area was
limited to about 6 cm2. The values of current used for
the two di†erent substrates were selected in order to
ensure a deposition potential greater than 800 mV with
respect to a saturated calomel electrode (SCE).
Electrochemical measurements

blend

electrolytes

artron (1287) electrochemical interface. The impedance
measurements were carried out in the frequency range
60 kHz to 10 mHz at a signal level of 20 mV, and the

polymer electrolytes was Ðrst optimized in order to
produce both conducting and mechanically stable Ðlms.
The optimum composition was (PEO)9 : LiCF3SO3
with a 50 wt% addition of PEG. The proton conducting
polymer electrolyte selected was the
previously reported by Petty-Weeks and Polak [19]
and a weight ratio of 60 : 40 of PVA and
chosen for the present study. Studies on the conductivi-
ty variation of these electrolytes as a function of tem-
perature have been published by us elsewhere.8 The
polymer electrolytes show conductivities of the order of
about 10~4 to 10~3 S cm~1 at room temperature (25¡C).
This order of conductivity is acceptable for these appli-
cations because it o†ers low resistance when a thin Ðlm
form (say 100È200 lm cm~2) of the polymer electrolyte
is used in electrochemical capacitors.

Figure 1 shows the typical impedance spectra of the
di†erent capacitor cells. Polypyrrole systems show
capacitive behaviour with both the proton and lithium
ion conducting polymer electrolytes. In particular, the
cell comprising polypyrrole deposited on ITO with the
Fig. 3. Discharge curves of di†erent capacitor cells for di†er-
impedance behaviour (Fig. 1 curve a) which is very close
to ideal capacitive characteristics. It should be noted
that an ideal impedance response for a pure capacitor is
a straight line parallel to the imaginary axis. However, a
slight deviation from such ideal characteristics has been
observed for the remainder of the capacitor cells. The
electrical parameters such as bulk resistance
resistance R and capacitance C values (evaluated from
eqn (1)) at frequencies 100 mHz and 10 mHz are sum-
marized in Table 1. The values of
ated from the intercept of the impedance curves with
real axis in the high frequency region. The capacitance
value of approximately 3É2 mF cm~2 (equivalent to
single electrode speciÐc capacitance of about 84 F g~1 of
polypyrrole) was observed for the cell with polypyrrole
(on ITO) electrode and proton conducting polymer
electrolyte. This corresponds to an energy density of
approximately 12 W h kg~1 (calculated for a maximum
working voltage of 1É0 V for the polypyrrole device).
Capacitance values for the cells with polypyrrole depos-
ited on carbon sheet are larger than those for the cells
made from polypyrrole deposited on ITO for both elec-
trolytes (Table 1). The large capacitance values of these
carbon substrate-based cells are also apparent from the
results of the cyclic voltammetry and chargeÈdischarge
experiments (described below). This is because the
carbon sheet has a comparatively larger surface area
due to its microsporous structure. Thus it provides a
larger e†ective area for polypyrrole deposition com-
pared to ITO, and hence an enhancement in capac-
Figure 2 represents the cyclic voltammograms of dif-
ferent capacitor cells at di†erent voltage scan rates.

With the exception of the cell based on the lithium ion
conducting polymer electrolyte and polypyrrole depos-
ited on carbon sheet the cyclic voltammograms are fea-
tureless. This indicates that the charge transfer between
polypyrrole and the electrolytes occurs at constant
rate.9 The small peaks observed in cathodic and anodic
regions for the cell based on the lithium ion conducting
polymer electrolyte and polypyrrole on carbon sheet are
an indication of possible insertion and depletion of
lithium ion through the porous structure of the elec-
trodes. The response for each cell has been found to be
dependent on scan rate and this is expected for a capa-
citor cell.22 The voltammograms are close to the ideal
rectangular shape at low scan rates (10È40 mV s~1). In
the case of capacitor cells with proton conducting
polymer electrolytes, the rectangular shape of the volt-
ammograms was observed even at rather high scan
rates (100È200 mV s~1). The capacitance values evalu-
Fig. 4. Discharge capacitance of di†erent cells as a function of
ated from the ratio of current to the sweep rate are in
good agreement with the values evaluated from imped-
ance measurements and the chargeÈdischarge method
been performed for all the capacitor cells. Figure 3
shows the typical discharge characteristics of the di†er-
ent capacitor cells at di†erent discharge currents. All the
cells were charged up to 1É0 V, because in the case of
polypyrrole systems the working potential is limited to
a maximum 1É0 V, which is the maximum potential for
doping and dedoping of polypyrrole electrodes.14,15
From the electrolyte point of view, the working poten-
tial of lithium ion conducting polymer is about 3É0 V,
whereas the working potential of proton conducting
polymer is limited to 1É2 V.8 Therefore, the proton con-
present study, in addition to the lithium ion conducting
polymer, is a highly suitable electrolyte for polypyrrole
after di†erent chargeÈdischarge

The behaviour of a real capacitor can be represented
by an equivalent circuit consisting of an ideal capacitor
connected in series with the internal resistance
initial drop in voltage while discharging the cells at con-
stant current is because of an ohmic loss across the

capacitance (evaluated from eqn (2)), internal resistance
and the coulombic efficiency (g) (calculated from eqn
The variation of discharge capacitance of di†erent
capacitor cells as a function of cycle number is shown in
Fig. 4. Each capacitor cell shows almost stable constant
values for up to 1000 chargeÈdischarge cycles. A sharp
decrease in the capacitance values for the initial few
cycles has been observed predominantly for the system
with the proton conducting polymer electrolyte and
polypyrrole electrode on carbon sheet. This initial
decrease in capacitance is possibly related to the irre-
bound surface groups (most likely OH~ groups) on the
porous polypyrrole on carbon sheet. The stability of the
cells with respect to various chargeÈdischarge cycles can
be seen from Fig. 5. The impedance pattern of the cell
(taking as an example of C o pPy o PVAÈH3PO4 o pPy o C
system) has been found to be almost una†ected after
several chargeÈdischarge cycles.

PEG) having room temperature conductivity

able electrolytes for the fabrication of conducting
polymer based redox supercapacitors. The capacitors
